question: Is a TP4056-class linear charger acceptable for the v0.1 power system, and under what conditions?
context: Generic TP4056 boards are cheap and ubiquitous, but the task explicitly forbids assuming they provide safe power-path management, load sharing, correct USB-C negotiation, battery protection, thermal protection, or safe charge-while-operate behavior. Each of those properties must be an explicit implementation decision.

alternatives.description: TP4056-class charger accepted ONLY together with: (1) DW01A-class battery protection present AND a protected cell (double protection accepted); (2) an added P-MOSFET load-sharing power path so the system load is not summed into charge-termination current; (3) 5.1 kOhm pull-downs on both USB-C CC pins; (4) program current set to 500 mA; (5) charger thermal behavior bench-verified inside the closed enclosure (validation-plan test 2); (6) charge-while-operate verified as part of test 2.
alternatives.tradeoffs: Pros: lowest cost, locally available. Cons: linear charging dissipates up to ~1 W at 500 mA; conditions add 3-4 BOM lines; termination accuracy depends on the load-sharing circuit actually working.

alternatives.description: A charger IC with integrated power path (BQ24074-class).
alternatives.tradeoffs: Pros: correct-by-construction load sharing and thermal regulation. Cons: higher unit cost and lower local availability for small batches (unverified); finer-pitch package raises hand-assembly risk.

rationale: v0.1 accepts the TP4056-class part strictly under the six enumerated conditions. The conditions are obligations on the implementation, not assumptions about the module: until validation-plan test 2 evidence exists, the charger-related constraints (con-sec-component-temp-85, con-sec-touch-temp-48) remain requires_validation and the rule kernel will not report them as pass.
tradeoffs: Accepts added circuit complexity (power path) in exchange for cost; accepts that a failed test 2 forces migration to the integrated-power-path alternative.
